sub_problems.description: Determine the specific type of website boilerplate needed (e.g., static HTML/CSS, React, Vue, Django, WordPress theme, etc.)
constraints: Must cover common web development scenarios

sub_problems.description: Identify required boilerplate components based on website type (e.g., HTML structure, CSS reset, navigation templates, responsive design setup)
constraints: Components must be minimal yet functional

sub_problems.description: Create language-appropriate boilerplate code (Greek interface vs. English code)
constraints: Code must be syntactically correct and follow best practices

sub_problems.description: Provide implementation instructions for the boilerplate
constraints: Instructions must be clear for developers of varying experience

sub_problems.description: Ensure boilerplate meets modern web standards (accessibility, performance, SEO basics)
constraints: Must include essential modern web requirements

assumptions.text: User wants actual code boilerplate, not just conceptual templates
assumptions.label: HYPOTHESIS
assumptions.rationale: The Greek word 'boilerplate' could refer to conceptual templates, but in web development context usually means reusable code

assumptions.text: User understands basic web development concepts
assumptions.label: UNKNOWN
assumptions.rationale: No information about user's technical background provided

assumptions.text: User wants a general-purpose boilerplate rather than domain-specific (ecommerce, blog, etc.)
assumptions.label: HYPOTHESIS

assumptions.text: Greek language in request doesn't require Greek-language boilerplate output
assumptions.label: VERIFIED
assumptions.rationale: Web development boilerplate code is typically in English regardless of interface language

assumptions.text: User wants production-ready boilerplate, not just learning examples
assumptions.label: HYPOTHESIS
assumptions.rationale: The term 'boilerplate' implies foundation for real projects

failure_modes: Providing overly specific boilerplate that doesn't match user's actual technology stack
Creating boilerplate that's too minimal (missing essential features) or too bloated (including unnecessary dependencies)
Assuming modern JavaScript frameworks when user might need plain HTML/CSS
Missing critical setup instructions that make boilerplate unusable
Failing to account for different deployment environments (local dev vs. production)

candidates.content: The problem analysis fundamentally misdiagnoses the request's ambiguity as a technical challenge rather than a communication failure. 'δώσε boilerplate για ιστοσελίδες' contains three critical ambiguities: 1) 'δώσε' (give) could mean provide code, recommend existing templates, or create custom solutions, 2) 'boilerplate' is undefined—could mean starter templates, code snippets, or full project scaffolds, 3) 'ιστοσελίδες' (websites) encompasses everything from static landing pages to complex web applications. The sub-problems assume a linear technical solution path when the actual failure mode is requirement gathering. SP1's constraint 'must cover common web development scenarios' is impossible—there are hundreds of valid 'common' scenarios with conflicting requirements. SP3's Greek interface vs English code constraint creates an unexamined localization problem: should comments, variable names, or UI text be in Greek? The assumption that Greek request doesn't require Greek output ignores that boilerplate often includes documentation and UI text. SP5's 'essential modern web requirements' is undefined—accessibility standards alone (WCAG AA vs AAA) represent fundamentally different implementation efforts. The entire analysis assumes the user wants to build something rather than possibly evaluate existing boilerplates or understand boilerplate concepts.
key_insights: The request's Greek origin creates unexamined encoding and character set requirements—boilerplate must handle UTF-8 properly, but many default templates assume Latin character sets, potentially breaking Greek content.
Boilerplate lifespan is ignored—modern web standards change monthly; any boilerplate becomes outdated within weeks unless maintained, creating technical debt from day one.

The analysis assumes a single 'correct' boilerplate exists, but boilerplate quality is subjective—what one developer considers minimal (SP2 constraint) another considers incomplete; there's no objective measure of 'essential' components.

candidates.content: The request for 'boilerplate for websites' in Greek reveals a fundamental misunderstanding of modern web development ecosystems. The sub-problems framework assumes a linear, deterministic approach to boilerplate generation, but this ignores the emergent reality that boilerplate itself has become an anti-pattern in professional development. The systemic issue is that boilerplate represents technical debt at inception—it's frozen architecture that doesn't evolve with the project's actual needs. Modern development has shifted toward scaffolding tools (Create React App, Vite, Next.js CLI) that generate optimized starting points with built-in build systems, testing frameworks, and deployment configurations. The deeper problem is that providing static boilerplate code creates immediate version lock-in, dependency management issues, and forces developers into architectural decisions before understanding their actual requirements. The Greek language aspect is particularly revealing—it suggests either a non-technical user seeking templates or a developer working in a specific regional context where English-language documentation creates accessibility barriers. The most significant systemic failure here is treating boilerplate as a product rather than recognizing it as a symptom of inadequate tooling ecosystems. In mature development environments, the 'boilerplate problem' has been abstracted away by framework CLI tools and starter kits that remain connected to upstream updates.
key_insights: Boilerplate requests often indicate missing meta-tools—the user likely needs guidance toward framework-specific generators rather than static code snippets, creating a dependency on your one-time solution rather than sustainable development practices.

The Greek interface/English code dichotomy creates cognitive load that framework documentation in English exacerbates, suggesting the real need might be localized developer education resources rather than code templates.

Modern web standards (SP5) have become so complex that no static boilerplate can remain compliant—accessibility, performance, and SEO requirements evolve monthly, making any fixed template immediately obsolete and potentially dangerous if followed uncritically.

tests.failure_mode: No solution exists to test. The candidate solutions array is empty, so there is no boilerplate code to execute, no server to run, no HTML/CSS/JS to render, and no functionality to operate. All assumptions about having a working website implementation fail immediately.
tests.recovery_path: 1. Define at least one concrete boilerplate solution (e.g., basic HTML5 template, React/Vue starter, static site generator config). 2. Implement the chosen solution with actual code files. 3. Deploy the implemented solution to a hosting environment. 4. Verify the website loads and functions as intended.

tests.failure_mode: Core assumption of having any solution breaks completely. With no code, resources like servers, databases, or CDNs cannot be utilized because there's nothing to host. Partial data (like incomplete templates) doesn't exist. The system cannot function at any level—no HTML to serve, no assets to load, no backend to query.
tests.recovery_path: 1. Acknowledge the empty solutions array as a critical design flaw. 2. Conduct requirements gathering to determine needed boilerplate features (e.g., responsiveness, SEO, performance). 3. Develop a minimal viable boilerplate (e.g., index.html with basic structure, style.css, script.js). 4. Test the boilerplate under resource constraints (e.g., low bandwidth, limited CPU) to ensure baseline functionality. 5. Document constraints and fallbacks (e.g., CSS Grid fallback for older browsers).

tests.failure_mode: An intelligent actor exploits the absence of any solution. Since there is no code to attack, the adversary trivially 'succeeds' by preventing any website from being created. They may block deployment attempts, poison development tools, or social-engineer the team to keep the solutions array empty. The failure is absolute—no value is delivered.
tests.recovery_path: 1. Treat the empty solutions array as a security incident (sabotage or oversight). 2. Implement version control and code reviews to ensure solutions are committed. 3. Use integrity checks (e.g., CI/CD pipelines that fail if no source code is detected). 4. Develop adversarial testing plans for any new boilerplate (e.g., stress-test with malicious input, DDoS simulations). 5. Establish rollback procedures and backups for boilerplate code.
